title: Significance tests: Parametric & non-parametric tests
body_html:
Statistical tests
Statistical (significance) tests help determine whether observed differences in data are likely to be due to chance. These tests generate a test statistic, which is used to calculate a p-value. If the p-value is below a pre-set threshold (typically 0.05), the result is considered statistically significant.

Choosing the correct statistical test depends on several factors:
  • Type of data: categorical vs. numerical (quantitative)
  • Distribution of the data: normal (Gaussian) or non-normal
  • Number of groups being compared
  • Pairing of data: paired (related measurements from the same individuals) vs. unpaired (independent groups)

In practice, these calculations are usually performed using statistical software, but understanding the test selection is crucial for interpretation. Tests are often described as being parametric or non-parametric. Parametric tests are used when data are continuous, normally distributed, and measured on an interval or ratio scale (e.g., blood pressure, weight). Non-parametric tests are used for skewed data, ordinal data (e.g., Likert scales), or small sample sizes. Also used for categorical data in frequency comparisons.

Parametric vs. Non-Parametric Tests
FeatureParametric TestsNon-Parametric Tests
AssumptionsRequires data to be normally distributed and measured on an interval or ratio scaleNo assumption of normality; suitable for skewed data or ordinal data
Basis of comparisonCompare meansCompare medians or ranks
PowerMore powerful if assumptions are metLess powerful, but more robust if assumptions are violated
Examples
  • Student's t-test - paired or unpaired*
  • Pearson's product-moment coefficient - correlation
  • Mann-Whitney U test - unpaired data
  • Wilcoxon signed-rank test - compares two sets of observations on a single sample
  • chi-squared test - used to compare proportions or percentages
  • Spearman, Kendall rank - correlation

    • *paired data refers to data obtained from a single group of patients, e.g. measurement before and after an intervention. Unpaired data comes from two different groups of patients, e.g. comparing response to different interventions in two groups

    Common tests based on data type and study design

    1. Comparing two groups
    Data TypeUnpairedPaired
    ParametricUnpaired Student’s t-test: compares means between two independent groupsPaired t-test: compares means before and after intervention in the same group
    Non-parametricMann–Whitney U test: compares medians of two independent groupsWilcoxon signed-rank test: compares medians of paired observations


    2. Comparing more than two groups
    Data TypeUnpairedPaired
    ParametricOne-way ANOVA: compares means of three or more independent groupsRepeated Measures ANOVA: compares means across multiple time points or conditions in the same group
    Non-parametricKruskal–Wallis test: compares medians of three or more independent groupsFriedman test: compares medians across multiple paired conditions

    3. Tests for categorical data
    All of the tests below are non-parametric tests
    TestUse Case
    Chi-squared (χ²) testCompares proportions between two or more independent groups (e.g., stroke rates in patients on vs. off statins)
    Fisher’s Exact TestUsed instead of Chi-squared when sample sizes are small (expected frequency < 5 in any cell)
    McNemar’s TestCompares proportions in paired categorical data (e.g., before and after a treatment within the same subjects)

    Key Definitions:
    • Paired data: Repeated measures on the same subjects (e.g., pre- and post-treatment)
    • Unpaired data: Data from different groups of individuals
    • Normal distribution: Symmetrical, bell-shaped distribution; required for parametric tests
    • Ordinal data: Categorical data with a meaningful order but not equal spacing (e.g., MMSE scores)
    Mayo clinic00
    Parametric and Nonparametric: Demystifying the Terms
    Parametric and Nonparametric Statistical Tests
    Dr Todd Grande - YouTube00
